request.question: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?

title: Clay County Savings Association Building
Edina Double Square Historic District
Bushwhacker
Clay County Courthouse (Alabama)
Claycomo, Missouri
Savings Bank Building
Old Clay County Courthouse (Clay, West Virginia)
Onondaga County Savings Bank
Clay County, Tennessee
Clay County Courthouse (Illinois)
sentences: The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.
The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.
The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.
Edina Double Square Historic District is a national historic district located at Edina, Knox County, Missouri.
The district encompasses 37 contributing buildings in the central business district of Edina.
It developed between about 1865 and 1945 and includes representative examples of Italianate and Streamline Moderne style architecture.

Notable contributing buildings include the Public Works Administration funded Knox County Courthouse (1934-1935) designed by William B. Ittner, Bishoff Bakery (1891), Northern Hotel (1860s), Ennis House/Northern Hotel (c. 1865), Edina School and Gymnasium (1915-1916), D. H. Mudd Building (c. 1904), Phillip Linville Building (c. 1908, c. 1963), Tobias J. Lycan Building (c. 1891), Jacob Pugh Building (c. 1891), Albert G. Bostick Building (c. 1891), Knox County Savings Bank (c. 1891), Thomas Burk Buildings (c. 1881), Bank of Edina Building (c. 1907), Joseph F. Biggerstaff Buildings (c. 1881), Stablein Building (c. 1870), and Knox County Public Library (c. 1915).

Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.
This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.
The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.
The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.
The Clay County Courthouse is a historic courthouse building in Ashland, Clay County, Alabama.
The Classical Revival-style building has served as the county courthouse since its completion in 1906.
Notable characteristics include its large dome, with clocks inset on four sides, and cupola surmounted by a statuary representing justice.
It was added to the National Register of Historic Places on November 21, 1976.
Claycomo is a village in Clay County, Missouri, United States.
The village’s name is a portmanteau of "Clay, Co" (Clay County), and "Mo" (Missouri).
The population was 1,430 at the 2010 census.
Ford Motor Company has an assembly plant in Claycomo.
Kansas City Assembly is responsible for building the Ford F-150 pickup trucks and the Ford Transit vans.
The Savings Bank Building is a commercial building located at 101 South Front Street in Marquette, Michigan.
It is also known as the Marquette County Savings Bank.
The building was designated a Michigan State Historic Site in 1976 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978.
The Old Clay County Courthouse in Clay, West Virginia was designed by Frank L. Packard and built in 1902.
The Beaux-Arts building was located on a hill overlooking the county seat.

The courthouse was the site of three notable trials: the Sarah Ann Legg trial of 1905, the first trial of a woman in Clay County for murder, the Booger Hole trial of 1917, in which citizens nearly lynched the defendants, and the Oscar Bail trial of 1953, in which Bail was convicted of killing a mine guard in the Great Widen Coal Strike.
The Onondaga County Savings Bank was chartered in 1855 in Syracuse, New York.
It was a franchise ahead of its time and had four separate branches by the late 19th century.
The "Onondaga County Savings Bank Building" was constructed in 1896 adjacent to the Erie Canal in "Clinton Square".
Clay County is a county located in the U.S. state of Tennessee.
As of the 2010 census, the population was 7,861.
Its county seat and only incorporated city is Celina.
Clay County is named in honor of American statesman Henry Clay, member of the United States Senate from Kentucky and United States Secretary of State in the 19th century.
Its current mayor is Dale Reagan.
The Clay County Courthouse, located at 300 Broadway Street in Louisville, is the county courthouse serving Clay County, Illinois.
Built in 1913, the courthouse was Clay County's fourth courthouse; it has served continuously as the seat of Clay County government since its opening.
Architect Joseph W. Royer, who planned several other Illinois courthouses, designed the Classical Revival building.
The courthouse was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2015.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?
hop1_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

Aryan Republican Army | The Aryan Republican Army is the name given to white supremacist criminal group active in the United States in the early to mid-1990s.  The group is alleged to have associated with convicted bomber Timothy McVeigh in the months before the Oklahoma City bombing.  The organization was sometimes referenced in the media as the Midwest Bank Robbers.  The group was created by Peter Langan and Richard Lee Guthrie in 1992.  The group considered themselves a leaderless organization, meaning that there was no true leader to the group and that they were more of a group of associated people acting together to achieve their goals.  Although the group was mainly a criminal enterprise, they did have a terrorist like agenda.  With the money they were able to steal in the 22 confirmed robberies they were involved in, the group began stockpiling weapons and ammunition.  It is believed that the group wished to start a race war with the weapons that were stockpiled.  The group began to fall apart as members of the Aryan Republican Army were arrested after one of the former members of the group became an informant as part of a plea bargain.  By mid-1996, practically all known members had been apprehended and the group was in shambles.

State Line Mob | The State Line Mob was an association of criminal elements that operated in the 1950s and 1960s at the Mississippi–Tennessee state line in Alcorn County, Mississippi, and McNairy County, Tennessee, along U.S. Route 45.  The State Line Mob was involved in bootlegging, gambling, prostitution, tourist fleecing, robbery, and murder.  A few of the members were from Phenix City, Alabama, having been displaced from that town when martial law was declared by the Governor and the Alabama National Guard attempted to clean the town up.

Florencia 13 | The Florencia 13 is an American criminal street gang in Los Angeles, California, composed mainly of Hispanic-Americans and some blacks.  The gangs name is after the Florence area of Los Angeles county, the 13 is for it's allegiance to the Mexican Mafia.  They are involved in drug smuggling, murder, assault, robbery etc.

Arifs (gang) | The Arifs are a South East London-based Turkish Cypriot criminal organization heavily involved in armed robbery, contract killing, drug trafficking and other racketeering-related activities within London's underworld since the late 1960s.  Following the downfall of the Kray brothers, the Arifs were one of several criminal organizations who took control of the London underworld including the Clerkenwell crime syndicate and the Brindle family with whom they were engaged in a highly publicized gangland war during the 1990s.

Quaker Springs, New York | Quaker Springs is a hamlet in Saratoga County.  The zip code is 12871.  Quaker Springs is between NY-32 Route 4.  Quaker Springs United Methodist Church is located in the hamlet.  An anti-slavery society was based in Quaker Springs and several possible participants in the Underground Railroad were in Quaker Springs.  The name Quaker Springs came from a type of mineral spring located in the area.  The area including Quaker Springs was involved in Revolutionary War positions.
U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II | U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
hop1_titles: U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Clay County Savings Association Building robbery: passage [1] says the robbery "was conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas" (passage [1]) and links to the "James–Younger Gang" (passage [1], quoted token "James–Younger Gang").
  - Named individuals mentioned as possible leaders: "Jesse James" and "Archie Clement" (passage [1]).
- Ambiguities:
  - The passages do not state any Revolutionary War–derived label explicitly. Multiple candidate labels could fit (e.g., "partisan", "bushwhacker", "Tory"/"Loyalist"), but none of those terms or variants appear in the supplied passages. Do not assume which is intended.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as given in the passages): "former Confederate guerrillas" (passage [1]) and the "James–Younger Gang" (passage [1]).
  - Missing fact needed: the exact criminal label that is "named after a term from the Revolutionary War" (the precise term and the page that states those robbers were called that).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open "Clay County Savings Association Building" full article — check the lede and "Robbery" or "History" sections and citations for any alternate labels applied to the robbers (look for terms such as "bushwhacker", "partisan", "guerrilla", or "Tory").
     - Search keywords: Clay County Savings Association Building lede/robbery "former Confederate guerrillas" aliases.
  2) Open "James–Younger Gang" page — check the lede, "Origins"/"Aliases"/"Members" sections for any Revolutionary War–derived label applied to the gang.
     - Search keywords: "James–Younger Gang aliases partisans bushwhackers".
  3) Open pages for candidate Revolutionary War terms to compare usage: "Partisan (military)", "Bushwhacker", and "Tory (American Revolution)" — check ledes and history/usage sections for later application to post‑Revolution robbers.
     - Search keywords: "bushwhacker Missouri", "partisan American Revolution usage", "term derived from Revolutionary War used for criminals".
  4) If needed, open "Jesse James" and "Archie Clement" pages for descriptions/epithets that might use the Revolutionary War term.
     - Check lede and "Early life/Career" or "Legacy" sections.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Clay County Savings Association robbers called bushwhackers
hop2_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

Bushwhacker | Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.  This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.  The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.  The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.

Whitfield v. United States | Whitfield v. United States, 574 U.S. ___ (2015) , was a United States Supreme Court that concerned whether the forced accompaniment statute under applies when a bank, credit union, or savings/loan association robber, or attempted robber, forces someone to accompany them for any distance.  Defense attorney Joshua B. Carpenter argued on behalf of the Petitioner, and Assistant to the Solicitor General Brian H. Fletcher argued on behalf of the Department of Justice.

Johnny Hope | John "Johnny" Hope (1856–1906) was a 19th-century American burglar, bank robber and pickpocket.  The son of James "Old Jimmy" Hope, he was alleged to have been associated with his father and the George Leslie Gang.  He was among those arrested for the 1878 robbery of the Manhattan Savings Institution although he and Billy Kelly were the only men actually sent to prison for this crime.
Haddadus binotatus | Haddadus binotatus (common name: clay robber frog) is a species of frog in the Craugastoridae family.  It is endemic to Brazil.

Jesse James | Jesse Woodson James (September 5, 1847April 3, 1882) was an American outlaw, bank and train robber, guerrilla, and leader of the James–Younger Gang.  Raised in the "Little Dixie" area of western Missouri, James and his family maintained strong Southern sympathies.  He and his brother Frank James joined pro-Confederate guerrillas known as "bushwhackers" operating in Missouri and Kansas during the American Civil War.  As followers of William Quantrill and "Bloody Bill" Anderson, they were accused of participating in atrocities against Union soldiers and civilian abolitionists, including the Centralia Massacre in 1864.

Clay County, Minnesota | Clay County is a county located in the U.S. state of Minnesota.  As of the 2010 census, the population was 58,999.  Its county seat is Moorhead.  The county's name is in honor of American statesman Henry Clay, member of the United States Senate from Kentucky and United States Secretary of State in the 19th century.  The county was formed in 1862 and organized in 1872.  It was originally called Breckenridge County, but changed to its current name in 1862.
response.summary_2: former Confederate guerrillas. Jesse Woodson James joined pro‑Confederate guerrillas known as "bushwhackers", a term used since the American Revolutionary War.
